title: The Widow's Two Coins
subtitle: Christ watching the treasury, as a poor widow gives her whole living

stillness: Be still, and sit a while.
God does not weigh how much you give,
but out of how much it is offered.
Quiet your heart, for He looks not at the gift, but at the giver's love.
gospel: And Jesus, sitting over against the treasury,
watched how the crowd cast money into it.
And many that were rich cast in much.
But there came a poor widow,
and she put in two small coins,
And He said: Amen I say to you,
this poor widow has cast in more than all.
For all these of their abundance cast into the offerings of God;
but she of her want has cast in all she had,
behold: Behold Jesus, seated over against the treasury,
watching how the crowd casts its money into it.
See the many rich, casting in much, and great gifts.
And see, coming among them, a poor little widow,
putting in two small coins of bronze,
which with her sweat she had earned for her daily bread.
She holds back nothing necessary to her body,
committing herself henceforth wholly to God.
The others gave from their surplus, from what overflowed;
she gave from her want, all that she had.
consider: The greedy priests taught that whoever offered more in the temple had the more merit;
but this is false.
The measure of merit is not taken from the size of the gift,
but by comparison with the means of the giver,
and the readiness of his will and devotion.
She gave more, not in weight, but in love;
not by the effect of the sum, but by the affection of devotion;
for what she could, she gave, and what she could not, she desired.
To give a little is more for the poor than to give much for the rich,
as it is more for a child to carry a small weight than for a strong man a great one.
Jerome says: God considers not how much, but out of how much you give.
Gregory says: God weighs the heart and not the substance.
This widow is the soul of every believer,
which must now reckon itself a widow,
so long as it is parted from the embraces of its bridegroom, Christ.
As a widow leads a mournful life and wears the garments of her widowhood,
so the devout soul must dwell in the grief of penitence,
humble in body and in mind.
She casts in two coins: her flesh and her mind —
the flesh by abstinence, the mind by humility,
laying down her whole living, leaving nothing of her own to the world.
The simple and the unlettered seize heaven,
while we with our learning are plunged into hell.
prayer: O God, be merciful to me, a sinner.
I am not as the proud, who trusting in their own justice
pray thus within themselves, as though not before You:
God, I give You thanks that I am not as the rest of men.
No — all that I live is not of my own merit, but of Your gift.
O God, be merciful to me, a sinner.

latinJewel.latin: Non quantum, sed ex quanto dederis, considerat Deus.
latinJewel.english: God considers not how much, but out of how much, you give.
